Publisher: Physics Letters B
Publication year: 2012
DOI: 10.1016/j.physletb.2012.08.020

The Story So Far

Short impact summary

The observation of a new particle in the search for the Standard Model Higgs boson by the ATLAS detector at the LHC has garnered significant academic attention, as evidenced by its 10,396 citations. This work, published in Physics Letters B, highlights extensive collaboration among prominent institutions including Lancaster University and New York University.

Full narrative

The research detailed in this article discusses the groundbreaking observation of a new particle in the pursuit of confirming the Standard Model Higgs boson. The study signifies a substantial contribution to the field of particle physics, evidenced by its remarkable citation count of 10,396. This extensive feedback from the academic community illustrates not only the importance of the findings but also the active discourse surrounding them, primarily in venues devoted to high-energy physics.

This work has notably received robust support from several key institutions, including Lancaster University, New York University, and Simon Fraser University. Such affiliations not only validate the quality of the research but also reflect its broad geographic footprint, with notable contributions from countries such as the United States, Germany, and Switzerland. This diverse institutional backing underlines the collaborative nature of cutting-edge research in particle physics.

An important aspect of this research is its alignment with rigorous scientific inquiry, as demonstrated by the proven downstream reuse of data and materials, which stands at 10,405 instances. This highlights a strong commitment to openness and accessibility in research outputs, further encouraging the next wave of inquiry and experimentation within this scientific domain. The incorporation of datasets may encourage other researchers to utilize these resources, fostering a culture of shared knowledge.

The comprehensive nature of the findings published in Physics Letters B indicates a significant step forward in understanding the fundamental particles and forces at play in the universe. As the field continues to advance, the implications of this research on theoretical and experimental physics will likely resonate for years to come while solidifying its role as a cornerstone in the ongoing exploration of particle physics.
